title: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact

items.subtopic: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)
items.title: HACEK Organisms' Association with Infective Endocarditis
items.content: HACEK organisms, particularly Aggregatibacter species, are strongly predictive of underlying infective endocarditis. The positive predictive value for endocarditis in HACEK bacteremia is 60%, but varies by organism.
keyPoints: Positive predictive value for endocarditis with HACEK bacteremia: 60%
Infective endocarditis diagnosed in 62–100% of Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans bacteremia cases
17–55% in Haemophilus parainfluenzae bacteremia, 50% with Cardiobacterium hominis bacteremia
0–6% of patients with Eikenella bacteremia develop endocarditis
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)

items.subtopic: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Complications of HACEK Endocarditis
items.content: HACEK endocarditis often presents subacutely, with Aggregatibacter or Cardiobacterium being more common. It is associated with embolic events in up to 71% of cases.
keyPoints: 0.8–6% of infective endocarditis cases are due to HACEK organisms
Subacute presentation, especially with Aggregatibacter or Cardiobacterium
Embolization prevalence: 28-71%
Valvular vegetations seen in up to 85% on echocardiography
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)

items.subtopic: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)
items.title: Diagnostic Approaches for HACEK Infections
items.content: HACEK infections require specific culture conditions due to slow growth. PCR and MALDI-TOF can aid in diagnosis, while ceftriaxone and levofloxacin are effective treatments.
keyPoints: Cultures positive within the first week with improved systems like BACTEC
PCR techniques (e.g., 16S rRNA) facilitate HACEK infection diagnosis
MALDI-TOF increases diagnostic accuracy and speed
Ceftriaxone and levofloxacin active against all isolates
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)

items.title: Cardiobacterium Species in Endocarditis
items.content: Cardiobacterium species, primarily C. hominis, are significant pathogens in endocarditis, especially affecting patients with pre-existing valvular heart disease or prosthetic valves. The aortic valve is most frequently involved.
keyPoints: C. hominis causes endocarditis mainly in those with underlying valvular issues.
The aortic valve is the most commonly affected site.
Complications include arterial embolization, mycotic aneurysms, and congestive heart failure.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · THE HACEK GROUP (part 2)

items.title: Kingella kingae Infections
items.content: Kingella kingae is a notable pathogen in pediatric bone and joint infections, with over half of cases involving these sites. It also causes endocarditis, bacteremia, and meningitis.
keyPoints: More than 50% of K. kingae infections are bone and joint infections.
Oropharyngeal colonization is highest in children under 3 years, detected in ~5–10%.
K. kingae bacteremia often presents with a petechial rash similar to Neisseria meningitidis sepsis.
It has surpassed Staphylococcus aureus as the leading cause of septic arthritis and osteomyelitis in children.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · THE HACEK GROUP (part 2)

items.subtopic: HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA
items.title: First-line Therapy and Treatment Duration for HACEK Endocarditis
items.content: Ceftriaxone is the first-line therapy for HACEK endocarditis, with an 80–90% favorable outcome. Levofloxacin can be considered as an alternative for patients intolerant to $\beta$-lactam therapy. Native-valve endocarditis requires 4 weeks of antibiotic treatment, while prosthetic-valve endocarditis necessitates 6 weeks. HACEK prosthetic-valve endocarditis often cures with antibiotics alone without surgical intervention.
keyPoints: Ceftriaxone (2 g/d) is first-line therapy for HACEK endocarditis
Favorable outcome in 80–90% of cases
Levofloxacin (750 mg/d) as an alternative for $\beta$-lactam intolerant patients
Native-valve endocarditis: 4 weeks of antibiotics
Prosthetic-valve endocarditis: 6 weeks of therapy
High cure rates with antibiotic treatment alone for HACEK prosthetic-valve endocarditis
1-year mortality significantly lower than infective endocarditis caused by viridans group Streptococcus
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA

items.subtopic: HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA
items.title: Clinical Features and Risks of Capnocytophaga Infections
items.content: Capnocytophaga species are part of the oral flora and can cause infections contiguous with the oropharynx, such as periodontal disease and respiratory tract infections. They have a high prevalence of resistance to $\beta$-lactams and macrolides. C. canimorsus and C. cynodegmi are associated with dog and cat bites, leading to severe sepsis in patients with predisposing conditions like asplenia or alcohol abuse. These infections may present with a petechial rash progressing to gangrene.
keyPoints: Capnocytophaga species part of oral flora
Associated with periodontal disease, respiratory tract infections, cervical abscesses
High resistance to $\beta$-lactams and macrolides
C. canimorsus and C. cynodegmi linked to dog/cat bites
Predisposing conditions: asplenia, glucocorticoid therapy, alcohol abuse
Severe sepsis with shock and disseminated intravascular coagulation possible
Petechial rash progressing from purpuric lesions to gangrene
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA

items.title: Empirical Treatment of Capnocytophaga Infections
items.content: For empirical treatment of infections caused by Capnocytophaga species, a penicillin derivative plus a $\beta$-lactamase inhibitor is recommended due to increasing $\beta$-lactamase production. If the isolate is susceptible, specific dosages are provided for effective treatment.
keyPoints: Ampicillin/sulbactam (1.5-3.0 g of ampicillin every 6 h) recommended due to increasing $\beta$-lactamase production.
For C. canimorsus, treat with penicillin (12-18 million units every 4 h) if susceptible.
Capnocytophaga is also susceptible to third-generation cephalosporins such as ceftriaxone (2 g every 12-24 h), doxycycline (100 mg every 12 h), and clindamycin (600-900 mg every 6-8 h).
Prophylactic antibiotics recommended for asplenic patients with dog-bite injuries.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Capnocytophaga Infections | Pasteurella multocida Infections

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Treatment of Pasteurella multocida Infections
items.content: Pasteurella multocida infections often affect skin and soft tissue, with a high rate of oropharyngeal colonization in cats and dogs. The organism can cause invasive diseases with systemic manifestations, especially in vulnerable populations.
keyPoints: Colonization rates are 50–90% in cats and 50–70% in dogs.
Most human infections affect skin and soft tissue; almost two-thirds caused by cats.
Increased risk of invasive infection with systemic manifestations in patients at extremes of age or with serious underlying disorders.
Mortality can be as high as 30% in invasive disease despite active antimicrobial therapy.
P. multocida is susceptible to penicillin, ampicillin, ampicillin/sulbactam, second- and third-generation cephalosporins, tetracyclines, and fluoroquinolones.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Capnocytophaga Infections | Pasteurella multocida Infections

items.subtopic: OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections
items.title: Achromobacter xylosoxidans: High Mortality and Treatment Challenges
items.content: Achromobacter xylosoxidans is a gram-negative bacterium associated with high mortality rates, particularly in immunocompromised patients. Nosocomial outbreaks are linked to contaminated fluids, and treatment relies on susceptibility testing due to common multidrug resistance.
keyPoints: Endogenous intestinal flora; isolated from water sources like well water, IV fluids, humidifiers
Increased risk for infection in immunocompromised hosts (e.g., cancer patients, postchemotherapy neutropenia)
Nosocomial outbreaks linked to contaminated fluids
Community-acquired bacteremia often occurs with pneumonia
Metastatic skin lesions present in one-fifth of cases
Reported mortality rate as high as 67%
Treatment based on susceptibility testing; multidrug resistance common
Typically active agents: Carbapenems, piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftazidime, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, minocycline, colistin
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections

items.subtopic: OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections
items.title: Aeromonas Species: Diverse Infection Sources and High Mortality in Specific Populations
items.content: Aeromonas species, particularly A. hydrophila, are associated with various infections, including sepsis and bacteremia, especially in immunocompromised hosts. They thrive in water sources and have been linked to severe outcomes post-hydrologic disasters.
keyPoints: Facultative anaerobic gram-negative bacterium
Common species: A. hydrophila, A. caviae, A. veronii, A. dhakensis
Proliferates in potable water, seawater, freshwater, raw seafood, soil
Controversial role in bacterial gastroenteritis; rare cases of hemolytic-uremic syndrome reported
Health care-associated sepsis and bacteremia in immunocompromised hosts
Community-acquired infections: bacteremia, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, biliary tract infections, skin/soft tissue infections
Severe soft tissue infections more common in Taiwan; prominent post-tsunami in Thailand
Associated with hydrologic disasters like floods
Mortality rates: 25% among immunocompromised adults with sepsis to >90% among patients with myonecrosis
A. dhakensis bacteremia linked to higher 14-day mortality rates
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections

items.title: Antibiotic Susceptibility and Resistance in Aeromonas Infections
items.content: Aeromonas species are typically susceptible to fluoroquinolones, third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, minocycline, and aminoglycosides. However, resistance has been reported, necessitating susceptibility testing for effective therapy guidance.
keyPoints: Aeromonas species are generally susceptible to fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin at 500 mg every 12 h PO or 400 mg every 12 h IV), third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems, minocycline, and aminoglycosides.
Resistance to these antibiotics has been described.
Susceptibility testing is essential due to Aeromonas' ability to produce various $\beta$-lactamases, including carbapenemases.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Aeromonas Infections

items.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis for Aeromonas in Specific Scenarios
items.content: When medicinal leeches are used, antibiotic prophylaxis with ciprofloxacin is recommended to prevent Aeromonas infections.
keyPoints: Antibiotic prophylaxis (e.g., with ciprofloxacin) is indicated when medicinal leeches are used.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Aeromonas Infections

items.title: Antibiotic Susceptibility and Resistance Patterns in Elizabet்கingia/Chryseobacterium Infections
items.content: Elizabet்கingia/Chryseobacterium infections are often susceptible to fluoroquinolones, minocycline, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole. They may also be susceptible to $\beta$-lactam/$\beta$-lactamase inhibitor combinations like piperacillin/tazobactam but show resistance to carbapenems. The rise of multi-drug-resistant isolates, including those with extended-spectrum $\beta$-lactamases and metallo-$\beta$-lactamases, necessitates susceptibility testing for optimal treatment.
keyPoints: Susceptible to fluoroquinolones, minocycline, trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole
May be susceptible to $\beta$-lactam/$\beta$-lactamase inhibitor combinations like piperacillin/tazobactam
Resistant to carbapenems
Increasing multi-drug-resistant isolates with extended-spectrum $\beta$-lactamases and metallo-$\beta$-lactamases
Combination therapy may be needed; perform susceptibility testing
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Elizabet்கingia/Chryseobacterium Infections | MISCELLANEOUS ORGANISMS

items.title: Clinical Context and Susceptibility of Rhizobium (formerly Agrobacterium) radiobacter Infections
items.content: Rhizobium (formerly Agrobacterium) radiobacter infections are typically associated with medical devices, such as intravascular catheters, prosthetic joints, valves, dialysis catheters, and post-cataract surgery endophthalmitis. These infections predominantly occur in immunocompromised hosts, including those with malignancy or HIV, and can also affect elderly patients with septic shock, watery diarrhea, and acute renal failure. Strains are usually susceptible to fluoroquinolones, third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins, and carbapenems.
keyPoints: Associated with medical devices like intravascular catheters, prosthetic joints/valves
Common in immunocompromised hosts (e.g., malignancy, HIV)
Also affects elderly patients with septic shock, diarrhea, acute renal failure
Susceptible to fluoroquinolones, third- and fourth-generation cephalosporins, carbapenems
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Elizabet்கingia/Chryseobacterium Infections | MISCELLANEOUS ORGANISMS

items.subtopic: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)
items.assertion: Prolonged incubation increases laboratory recovery of clinically significant HACEK isolates.
items.reason: Studies have not shown that prolonged incubation increases laboratory recovery of clinically significant HACEK isolates.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly states that studies have not shown increased recovery with prolonged incubation.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram-Negative Bacteria | THE HACEK GROUP (part 1)

items.subtopic: HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA
items.assertion: Cure rates for HACEK prosthetic-valve endocarditis are high.
items.reason: HACEK endocarditis is often cured with antibiotic treatment alone without surgical intervention.
items.explanation: The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · HACEK Endocarditis | OTHER FASTIDIOUS GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA

items.subtopic: OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections
items.assertion: A. caviae is associated with health care-related bacteremia.
items.reason: Aeromonas infections are more common in Taiwan and can occur after hydrologic disasters.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why A. caviae is associated with health care-related bacteremia.
items.reference: Infections Due to the HACEK Group and Miscellaneous Gram Negative Bact · OTHER GRAM-NEGATIVE BACTERIA | Achromobacter xylosoxidans Infections

items.assertion: Susceptibility testing should be performed for Elizabet்கingia/Chryseobacterium infections.
items.reason: This helps guide the choice of optimal antibiotic agents.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
